summary: Gruber responds to accusations of Apple-biased hypocrisy for criticizing Microsoft's 'Future Visions' concept video while supposedly giving Apple's 1987 'Knowledge Navigator' a pass. He argues that the same criticism applies equally to 1987 Apple, which was a broken company whose concept videos distracted from real products and contributed to its near-bankruptcy. He contends that today's Apple would never release such a video because it maintains relentless focus on shipping actual products people want to buy. The core argument is that publishing futuristic concept videos is a symptom of institutional lack of focus on the present, not a sign of visionary leadership. He draws a parallel between Microsoft's Windows Phone and Apple's Newton — both innovative but failing to gain market traction. He closes by noting that RIM also publishes concept videos, driving the point home with cutting sarcasm.
key_points: The criticism of Microsoft's concept video applies equally to 1987 Apple — 'Knowledge Navigator' was a symptom of a broken company that lost its way
Concept videos are not a cause of failure but a diagnostic sign of a company lacking institutional focus on present-day execution
Today's Apple never publishes concept videos because it channels all public attention toward actual shipping products
Microsoft is squandering attention on futuristic fantasies when it should be focused on products like Windows Phone that desperately need market traction
The companies that publish concept videos — 1987 Apple, Sun, AT&T, RIM — share a pattern of decline or irrelevance
money_quotes: "We're like Apple in 1987" is not a badge of honor — it's a flashing red warning light.
The designs in these concept videos are free from real-world constraints — technical, logical, fiscal. Dealing with constraints is what real design is all about.
I'm arguing that making concept videos is a sign of a company that has a lack of institutional focus on the present and near-present.
The attention of the public and the media is a rare and precious commodity. Apple today uses what attention it gets to focus on actual new products, ones that you can go out and actually buy and use.
Can you imagine a sports team in the midst of a present-day losing season that makes a video imagining a future championship 10 years out?
The Newton was a brilliant design and full of terrific industry-leading ideas. And it struck the market like a wet match.
"Knowledge Navigator" encapsulates everything that was wrong with Apple in 1987. Their coolest products were imaginary futuristic bullshit.
key_insight: Futuristic concept videos are not signs of visionary ambition but symptoms of companies that lack the institutional discipline to focus on shipping real products in the present.
